perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that, for a period of 5 years from receipt of information from the other party hereunder, such party shall use the same means it uses to protect its ow n confidential and propriet ary information to prevent the disclosure and to protect the confidentiality of both: i) written information received from the other party which is marked or identified as "confidential"; and ii) oral or visual information identified as confidential at the time of disclosure which is reduced to written form and provided to the other in such written form promptly after such oral or visual disclosure, hereinafter collectively referre d to as "the Information".
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that, for a period of approximately 5 years from receipt of information from the other party hereunder, such party shall use reasonably similar means to those it uses to protect its own confidential and proprietary information to prevent, where feasible, the disclosure and to generally protect the confidentiality of both: i) written information received from the other party which is marked or identified as "confidential"; and ii) oral or visual information identified as confidential at the time of disclosure which is reduced to written form and provided to the other in such written form promptly after such oral or visual disclosure, hereinafter collectively referred to as "the Information".
perturbation.explanation: The phrase "approximately 5 years" introduces ambiguity about the duration of confidentiality. Replacing "the same means it uses" with "reasonably similar means" weakens the protection standard. Adding "where feasible" before "disclosure" introduces a loophole for disclosures. Replacing "protect the confidentiality" with "generally protect the confidentiality" reduces the guarantee of confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, making it potentially non-compliant with the UK's Trade Secrets Regulations, which requires effective and consistent measures to protect trade secrets from unlawful acquisition, use or disclosure. The ambiguous language undermines the required standard of protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.justification: The change introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially conflicting with the UK's Trade Secrets Regulations, as stated in the Law Explanation. The phrase "approximately 5 years" makes the duration of confidentiality unclear, and "reasonably similar means" weakens the protection standard. The addition of "where feasible" introduces a loophole, and "generally protect the confidentiality" reduces the guarantee of confidentiality. While the snippets provided do not explicitly state trade secret regulations in the UK, the Law Explanation suggests that these ambiguities undermine the standard of protection required, making it difficult to enforce the original intent. The perturbation explanation clearly outlines how these changes weaken the original contract.

perturbation.original_text: Upon completion of the Business Purpose and/or upon written request of the party providing the Information, the other party shall within 14 days return all copies of th e Information to the providing party or certify in writing that all copies of the Information have been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon substantial completion of the Business Purpose and/or upon a request of the party providing the Information, the other party shall, when reasonably possible, within approximately 14 days, endeavor to return most copies of the Information to the providing party or, if convenient, provide some form of confirmation regarding the disposition of the Information.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'substantial completion' is vague. 'A request' weakens the demand. 'When reasonably possible' creates an escape clause for the timeframe. 'Approximately 14 days' softens the deadline. 'Most copies' and 'some form of confirmation regarding the disposition' further weaken the obligation to return or destroy all information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The changes create uncertainty around the deletion or return of data, potentially conflicting with the UK's Data Protection Act 2018, specifically Article 5(1)(e), which mandates that personal data be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates a clear and time-bound obligation regarding the return or destruction of confidential information. The changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation, using terms like 'substantial completion,' 'a request,' 'when reasonably possible,' 'approximately 14 days,' 'most copies,' and 'some form of confirmation.' This directly undermines the requirement to maintain data security, as the edited contract provides loopholes that might lead to non-compliance. The law explanation highlights a potential conflict with the UK's Data Protection Act 2018, Article 5(1)(e), which requires that personal data be kept only as long as necessary. Though scraped snippets provide limited direct support, the perturbation explanation and law explanation when coupled with the changes to the contract language, indicate that the modified text introduces significant ambiguity, making it hard to enforce data protection standards, potentially resulting in a legal violation.

perturbation.original_text: No media releases, public announcements or public disclosure by either party relating to this Agreement, its subject matter or the Information, including but not limited to promotional or marketing, shall be made without the prior written consent of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: No media releases, public announcements or broad public disclosures by either party directly referencing this Agreement, its precise subject matter or specific details of the Information, particularly relating to core promotional or marketing aspects, should generally be made without considering obtaining the consent of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'broad' and 'directly referencing' limits the scope of the restriction. Replacing 'precise subject matter or specific details of the Information' narrows the restriction. Adding 'particularly relating to core promotional or marketing aspects' further narrows the restriction. Changing 'shall be made without the prior written consent' to 'should generally be made without considering obtaining the consent' makes the restriction non-mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: The changes weaken the control over information disclosure, which could conflict with the Market Abuse Regulation (MAR) Article 17, specifically insider information regulations. MAR requires precise control over information that could affect the market if released. The permissive language introduced could lead to selective or premature disclosure, potentially violating MAR's requirements for timely and complete information dissemination.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article III | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article III Article III refers to Article III of the Constitution , the section of the Constitution that authorizes a federal court system . This provides that federal courts have judicial power over all cases and controversies arising under the Constitution, laws, or treaties of the United States; of admiralty and maritime jurisdiction ; in which the United States is a party; between two or more states; between a state and the citizens of another state; between citizens of different states; between citizens of the same state claiming lands under grants of different states; and between a state or citizens thereof and foreign states, citizens, or subjects. An Article III judge is a judge who is authorized under Article III of the Constitution. An Article III court is a court that Congress establishes pursuant to Article III of the Constitution. [Last reviewed in March of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords constitutional law Wex admiralty/maritime CIVICS the Constitution COMMERCE commercial activities transportation THE LEGAL PROCESS courts judicial administration legal practice/ethics wex definitions constitutional law courts and procedure government legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the control over information disclosure. The original text mandates 'prior written consent,' while the changed text uses permissive language such as 'should generally be made without considering obtaining the consent,' making the restriction non-mandatory. This contradicts the Law Explanation's mention of Market Abuse Regulation (MAR) Article 17, requiring precise control over information. Neither of the scraped snippets are directly relevant.
